articles.title: Is It Really Rude to Correct Someone Who’s Wrong?
articles.content:
如果说我有一个致命缺点，那就是我必须是对的——尤其是当别人错了的时候。还是个孩子的时候，我在纠正别人这件事上简直是个麻烦精，要是你想知道这是不是交朋友的好方法，我可以告诉你：真的不是。所以随着年龄增长，我学会了闭紧嘴巴。有时候真的是字面意义上的咬舌头。尝点血和有朋友之间，我选择后者，因为很多时候，正确并不值得付出社交代价。

就说最近的一次晚宴吧，一个朋友自信满满地宣布我们只使用了10%的大脑。就这么说出来了，好像这是什么事实一样。这根本不是事实。作为一个报道过这个话题的科普作家，我能感觉到自己的眼皮开始抽动。这是最被彻底揭穿的健康谣言之一——脑部扫描显示几乎整个大脑都有活动——然而它就这样信心满满地在甜点时间被端了出来。我勉强沉默了大约45秒，然后就再也忍不住了。“实际上，”我说，“我写过一篇关于这个的文章……”他不肯退让，主要是因为他要用这个来支持他关于我们生活在一个模拟世界里的理论，而我是坚定的科学派，所以我们陷入了尴尬的僵局。桌子陷入了沉默。

当然，赌注并不总是这么高。我也曾因为纠正朋友反复错误引用《老友记》而变得极其迂腐，现在回想起来，那不是我最光彩的时刻。另一方面，我也确实纠正过人之后才发现，自己错了而对方是对的，这让我非常谦卑。我个人很感激这种情况发生，因为我真的宁愿知道真相，也不愿自信满满地四处走动却满是错误。然而有些人的偏好恰恰相反，而且这种人到处都是。

如果你在网上评论区待过，就知道这种纠正别人的冲动有多强烈。有些人已经把这件事变成了自己的全部个性。在这个“后真相社会”里，几乎不可能区分AI生成的兔子蹦床视频和真正可爱的宠物，似乎我们互相帮助就显得至关重要。但到底在哪里划线呢？让我们来讨论一下。

纠正最明显的论据是：错误信息会传播、生根并造成实际伤害。美国心理学会指出，未经质疑的虚假说法会被重复——虚假说法被重复得越多，就越令人信服。这就是所谓的“幻觉真相效应”。最终，如果你说得足够频繁、足够大声，人们就会相信那是真的。所以从这个角度来看，放任错误信息不是礼貌的克制，而是可能成为帮凶。

这同样适用于那些傻事。我喜欢网上那些帖子，人们分享他们小时候被教导的荒谬事情，并深信不疑地信了很多年。对我来说，是有一次我爸告诉我公路上白色分隔线上的凸起是为了让盲人能够开车——你知道，就是汽车的盲文。直到我三十多岁的时候，当着同事们的面说出这件事，我才突然意识到这有多蠢。我仍然记得朋友们震惊的表情。不只是因为我大错特错，而是因为我竟然以毕业典礼致告别词代表的身份大学毕业（还是毕业生代表！），却相信盲人通过触觉反馈在高速公路上导航。我深感尴尬，但很感激被纠正。你希望你的朋友也犯类似的错误吗？光是想想那种替人尴尬的感受，就应该促使我们互相帮助。

还有实际的考量。如果公司里有人在领导面前自信地呈报错误数据，为了避免尴尬而保持沉默可能会产生真正的后果。如果一个朋友重复一个医学谣言，沉默可能影响他们的健康决定。如果一个至亲的珍贵家庭故事是错的，可能导致数十年的感情伤害。语境至关重要，但主题本身也很重要。

还有关于尊严的论点：深思熟虑地纠正某人，实际上是一种尊重。这把他们当作能够处理准确信息的人，而不是需要被保护免受真相伤害的脆弱之人。而且人们往往比你想象的更容易接受。美国心理学会期刊《认知研究》发表的研究发现，人们通常会对事实纠正做出回应，而人们担心的“反效果”（被纠正后反而更加固执）实际上比之前认为的要少见得多。

事情在这里变得令人不舒服。纠正——即使是准确的纠正，即使是出于好意的纠正——都可能适得其反。而且最有可能发生的情况是：当纠正涉及事实以外的东西（比如感受），违背了根深蒂固的信念，感觉居高临下，来自被纠正者已经不信任的人，或者在众目睽睽之下被提出时。公开纠正增加了一层羞辱感，而被羞辱的人很少会说：“你说得对，我承认。”

还有双方面的邓宁-克鲁格问题。由康奈尔大学的心理学家大卫·邓宁和贾斯汀·克鲁格描述的这种效应，指的是对某个主题知之甚少的人，却有着学龄前儿童穿钢铁侠服装般的自信，而真正的专家却保持沉默，担心自己可能不知道的所有事情。这导致了非常特殊的争论：一方拥有事实、证据和多年的经验，另一方只有一个在叠衣服时半看的YouTube视频。在这些辩论中，最蠢的人通常仅仅通过坚持不懈就能获胜。所以你纠正的那个人可能完全错误却坚信自己是对的。

这里令人谦逊的部分：你也可能是这样的。在开口之前，值得诚实自我审视一下。你到底有多确定？

还有社交代价。即使你是对的，当众纠正某人也会让你看起来是在把正确置于与你在一起的人的感受之上。正如菲尔博士曾经说过的那句名言：“你是想正确还是想快乐？”尤其在群体场合，纠正别人可能被解读为权力展示或表演，或者让你看起来像个万事通。正如我亲身证明的那样，正确并不一定能让你受欢迎。

问自己以下五个问题，将帮助你放慢脚步，决定是否真的应该说出那些纠正的话。

1. 你和对方是什么关系？纠正一个亲密朋友与纠正一个陌生人、熟人、上司、约会对象或刚在晚宴上认识的人，是完全不同的情况。关系越亲近、越平等，纠正就越可能被视为关心而不是居高临下（当然要用适当的方式；下面会详细说）。对于一个熟人，同样的措辞可能被认为是攻击。对于上司，纠正可能被解读为不服从。

2. 话题是无害的还是敏感的？低风险的事实错误可以忽略。（电影是哪一年上映的真的很重要吗？）没有情感联系的关键事实通常最容易被接受——例如，“项目截止日期是这周五，不是下周末。”

然而，触及某人政治、宗教或根深蒂固的身份认同的纠正，是不同的领域——不是因为这些信仰不可能错误，而是因为纠正的社交代价要高得多，而成功的可能性要低得多。你没有义务放任危险的错误信息，但要知道“我不同意你对这位政治候选人的看法”和纠正一个虚假事实不是一回事。明智地花费你的社交资本。

3. 你的语气是什么？语气几乎比其他任何事情都重要。“哦，我实际上读到过不同的说法”和“你完全错了”之间有天壤之别。前者邀请对话；后者招致防御。将纠正框定为问题或你自己的不确定（“等等，不是……吗？”）往往比声明式的纠正更容易被接受，即使你100%确定。这也是与持相反观点的人交谈而不演变成争论的相同原则：以好奇心开头，而不是评判。

4. 你能当场谷歌一下吗？实时查证的能力是一种不可思议的力量，而且，像大多数超能力一样，也是一种潜在的危险武器。我们都经历过：有人说了一些可疑的话，而你口袋里就有一个设备，可以在11秒内解决争议。是否可以拿出来，完全取决于关系和气氛。在经常这么做的亲密朋友之间，没问题，甚至很有趣。在第一次约会时，抽出手机当场查证，可能会让对方拼命示意买单。谨慎行事。

5. 如果你错了怎么办？坦白说，这才能体现品格。如果你开口后发现自己是错的，你有两个选择：坚持到底（不好），或者优雅地让步（好）。“哦，你说得对！我搞错了——谢谢！”这真的是一个人能说的最不具防御性、最讨人喜欢的话之一。这也足够罕见，人们会记住它。

当你决定值得纠正时，以下是如何做到这一点而不成为每个人都害怕坐在旁边的那种人。

纠正一个错误的人本质上并不粗鲁，但如何做决定了它是有帮助的还是令人讨厌的。清单很简单：这真的重要吗？你确定吗？你是为了正确的理由吗？你能用“顺便说一句”的方式而不是红色按钮“错误”的方式来做吗？

目标不是赢。而是让每个人——包括你自己——都带着更准确的信息离开——理想情况下，每个人的尊严都完好无损。如果你被纠正了？你能做的最优雅的事情就是倾听、考虑，如果证据支持的话，更新你的看法。

能够被纠正比永远正确更令人印象深刻。知道什么时候该闭嘴是——作为一个在“大脑只用了10%”的评论后整整45秒就破功的人来说——一项重要的生活技能。一个我仍在努力的技能。但嘿，和我的童年相比，45秒后才打断别人已经是巨大的进步了！

有没有一个社交情境让你无法停止反复思考？发邮件到advice@tmbi.com，或者在Instagram上给夏洛特发消息@CharlotteHiltonAndersen。

《读者文摘》发表了数百篇礼仪故事，帮助读者在不断变化的世界中驾驭沟通。我们定期报道各种话题，如任何场合发送的最佳信息、看起来礼貌但实际上不那么礼貌的习惯、电子邮件和短信礼仪、商务礼仪、小费礼仪、旅行礼仪等等。我们致力于由具有专业知识和经验的作家提供高质量内容，并咨询相关合格专家。我们依靠政府、专业组织和学术机构等权威原始资料，以及我们作家在适当情况下的个人经验。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可靠来源支持，并随着时间推移重新审视，确保它们保持准确和最新。了解更多关于我们团队、贡献者和编辑政策的信息。
articles.summary:
If I have a fatal flaw, it is that I need to be right—especially when someone else is wrong. As a child, I was an absolute menace about correcting people, and in case you’re wondering whether that’s a great way to make friends, I can confirm it is not. So as I got older, I learned to bite my tongue. Sometimes literally. Tasting a little blood and having friends is preferable to the alternative, because quite often, being right is not worth the social cost.

Take a recent dinner party where a friend confidently announced that we only use 10% of our brains. Just said it, like it’s a fact. It is not a fact. As a science writer who has covered this topic, I could feel my eye beginning to twitch. This is one of the most thoroughly debunked health myths—brain scans show activity across virtually the entire brain—and yet here it was, being delivered with complete confidence over dessert. I managed to stay quiet for approximately 45 seconds before I could not take it anymore. “Actually,” I said, “I wrote an article about that …” He would not back down, mostly because he was using it to support his theory that we are all living inside a simulation, and as I am firmly Team Science, we came to an awkward impasse. The table went silent.

The stakes aren’t always this high, of course. I have also gotten extremely pedantic correcting a friend who kept misquoting Friends, which, in hindsight, was not my finest hour. And on the flip side, I have absolutely corrected people only to discover, with great humility, that they were right and I was wrong. I’m personally grateful when this happens, as I’d genuinely rather know the truth than walk around confidently incorrect. Some people have the opposite preference, however, and those people are everywhere.

If you’ve spent any time in a social media comments section, you already know how deep this urge to correct runs. Some people have made it their entire personality. And in a “post-truth society,” where it’s getting nearly impossible to differentiate between AI videos of bunnies jumping on a trampoline and real adorable pets, it seems imperative that we help each other out. But where, exactly, is the line? Let’s discuss.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more etiquette, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

The case for speaking up

The most obvious argument for correction is that misinformation spreads, takes root and causes actual harm. The American Psychological Association has noted that unchallenged false claims get repeated—and the more a false claim is repeated, the more believable it becomes. It’s a phenomenon known as the illusory truth effect. Eventually, if you say something often enough and loud enough, people believe it’s true. So from this view, letting misinformation slide isn’t polite restraint; it’s potentially complicit.

This applies to the silly stuff too. I love those online threads where people share the ridiculous things they were taught as a child that they believed far into adulthood. For me, it was when my dad told me that the white line divider bumps on the road were there so blind people could drive—you know, like braille for cars. And it didn’t even dawn on me how dumb that was until I said it out loud to a group of work colleagues when I was in my 30s. I still remember the shocked faces of my friends. Not just because I was oh-so-wrong, but because I had apparently graduated college (as the valedictorian, no less) while believing blind people were navigating the interstate via tactile feedback. I was deeply embarrassed but grateful to be corrected. Would you want your friend doing something similar? The secondhand embarrassment alone should motivate us to help each other out.

There are also practical considerations. If someone at work confidently presents incorrect data to leadership, staying quiet to avoid awkwardness could have real consequences. If a friend repeats a medical myth, silence could affect their health decisions. If a loved one’s cherished family story is wrong, it could lead to decades of hurt feelings. Context matters enormously, but so does the subject matter.

There’s also the dignity argument: Correcting someone, done thoughtfully, is actually a form of respect. It treats them as a person capable of handling accurate information, rather than a fragile person who needs to be protected from the truth. And people are often more open to it than you think. Research published in the journal Cognitive Research found that people are generally responsive to factual corrections, and the feared “backfire effect” (where being corrected causes someone to dig in harder) turns out to be far less common than previously thought.

The case against speaking up (and it’s a strong one)

Here’s where things get uncomfortable. Corrections—even accurate ones, even well-intentioned ones—can backfire. And it’s most likely to happen when the correction is addressing things other than clear facts (like feelings), goes against deeply held beliefs, feels condescending, comes from someone the person already distrusts or is delivered when others are watching. Public correction adds a layer of humiliation, and humiliated people rarely say, “You know what? Fair point.”

There’s also the Dunning-Kruger problem on both sides of the equation. Described by psychologists David Dunning and Justin Kruger at Cornell, this is the phenomenon where people who know very little about a subject are blessed with the confidence of a preschooler in an Iron Man costume, while actual experts stay quiet, worrying about all the things they might not know. This leads to a very special kind of argument in which one person has facts, evidence and years of experience, and the other has a YouTube video they half-watched while folding laundry. In these debates, the dumbest person usually wins simply by sheer persistence. So the person you’re correcting may be completely wrong and utterly convinced they’re right.

And here’s the humbling part: so might you. Before opening your mouth, it’s worth a beat of honest self-interrogation. How sure are you, really?

There’s also the social cost. Even when you are right, the act of correcting someone publicly can make you look like you’re prioritizing being right over the feelings of the person you’re with. As Dr. Phil once famously said, “Would you rather be right or be happy?” In group settings, especially, correcting someone can come across as a power move or a performance, or make you look like a know-it-all. As I can personally attest, being correct does not necessarily make you popular.

A few things to consider before you correct

Asking yourself these five questions will help you slow your roll and decide if you should actually utter those fateful words of correction.

1. What is your relationship to the other person? Correcting a close friend is very different than correcting a stranger, an acquaintance, a boss, a date or someone you’ve just met at a dinner party. The closer and more equal the relationship, the more a correction can land as caring rather than condescending (when said in an appropriate way; more on that below). With an acquaintance, the same words can read as an attack. With a boss, the correction can read as insubordination.

2. Is the topic innocuous or heated? Low-stakes factual errors can be overlooked. (Does it really matter what year the movie came out?) Crucial facts without any emotional connection are generally the best received—for example, “The project due date is this coming Friday, not next weekend.”

However, corrections that bump up against someone’s politics, religion or deeply held identity are different territory—not because those beliefs can’t be wrong, but because the social cost of the correction is much higher and the likelihood of success is much lower. You’re not obligated to let dangerous misinformation slide, but know that “I disagree with your take on this political candidate” is not the same thing as correcting a false fact. Spend your social capital wisely.

3. What is your tone? Tone matters more than almost anything else. There’s a world of difference between saying, “Oh, I actually read something different about that” and leading with “You’re completely wrong.” The first invites dialogue; the second invites defensiveness. Framing a correction as a question or as your own uncertainty (“Wait, wasn’t it … ?”) tends to land better than a declarative correction, even when you’re 100% certain. This is also, incidentally, the same principle behind talking to someone with an opposing viewpoint without it devolving into an argument: Lead with curiosity, not judgment.

4. Can you google it on the spot? The ability to fact-check people in real time is an incredible power and, like most superpowers, a potentially dangerous weapon. We’ve all been there: Someone says something dubious, and you have a device in your pocket that can resolve the dispute in 11 seconds. Whether it’s OK to pull it out depends entirely on the relationship and the vibe. Between close friends who do this all the time, it’s fine, even fun. On a first date, whipping out your phone to fact-check mid-sentence can have the other person frantically signaling for the check. Tread lightly.

5. What if you’re the one who’s wrong? This, frankly, is where character is revealed. If you speak up and it turns out you were the incorrect one, you have two options: Dig in (bad), or graciously concede the point (good). “Oh, you’re right! I had that wrong—thank you!” is genuinely one of the most disarming and likable things a person can say. It’s also rare enough that people remember it.

How to correct someone without being a jerk

When you’ve decided a correction is worth making, here’s how to do it and not become the person everyone dreads sitting next to.

  • Keep it private when possible. Pulling someone aside after the fact (“Hey, I wanted to mention I think that statistic might be off”) lands much better than correcting them in front of a group. It lets them save face, which people genuinely appreciate.
  • Make it about the information, not the person. “I think I read something different about this” is the start of a conversation, not a personal attack.
  • Pick your battles. Not every wrong thing needs to be corrected right now, or by you, or even at all. Ask yourself: Does this actually matter? Will correcting it help anyone? Or am I just annoyed that someone said a thing incorrectly? (The answer, if you’re anything like me, is sometimes the third one. But hey, at least we know that about ourselves!)
  • Be genuinely open to being wrong yourself. If you’re not willing to concede when you turn out to be incorrect, you don’t actually have the moral authority to correct anyone else.
  • Understand what’s really at stake. In public or private settings with high stakes, you have a duty to speak up, even if it’s uncomfortable. When someone is spreading misinformation that could lead to real harm—dangerous medical advice, incorrect safety information or fear-based myths that affect real decisions—staying quiet in the name of social comfort is bad etiquette. You might make an enemy. You might also prevent someone from making a very bad decision. The social rules exist for a reason, but they shouldn’t paralyze you when the situation genuinely warrants action.

The verdict

Correcting someone who’s wrong is not inherently rude, but how you do it determines whether it comes across as helpful or obnoxious. The checklist is simple: Does this actually matter? Are you sure? Are you doing it for the right reason? And can you do it in a way that’s more “by the way” than red-buzzer “INCORRECT”?

The goal isn’t to win. It’s to leave everyone, including yourself, with more accurate information—ideally, with everyone’s dignity left intact. And if someone corrects you? The most gracious thing you can do is listen, consider it and update your view if the evidence warrants it.

Being correctable is more impressive than being right. And knowing when to shut up is—and I say this as someone who let a 10%-of-your-brain comment go exactly 45 seconds before cracking—a vital life skill. One that I am still working on. But hey, interrupting people after 45 seconds is actually a huge improvement from my childhood. Progress!

Have a social situation you can’t stop ruminating on? Email us at advice@tmbi.com, or message Charlotte on Instagram at @CharlotteHiltonAndersen.

Why trust us

Reader’s Digest has published hundreds of etiquette stories that help readers navigate communication in a changing world. We regularly cover topics such as the best messages to send for any occasion, polite habits that aren’t as polite as they seem, email and texting etiquette, business etiquette, tipping etiquette, travel etiquette and more. We’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • American Psychological Association: “Recommendations for countering misinformation”
  • Journal of Personality and Social Psychology: “Unskilled and unaware of it: How difficulties in recognizing one’s own incompetence lead to inflated self-assessments”
  • Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications: “Examining the replicability of backfire effects after standalone corrections”
  • Nature Reviews Psychology: “The psychological drivers of misinformation belief and its resistance to correction”

Is It Rude to Share Someone Else's News?

Is It Rude to Buy Food for a Potluck?

The post Is It Really Rude to Correct Someone Who’s Wrong? appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: Would You Pay $6 Million for This Famous Jersey? Here’s What Has Sports Fans Salivating Right Now
articles.content:
体育纪念品是一项大生意。你能想到还有哪个市场，有人汗湿的旧运动短裤能卖出数千甚至数百万美元吗？这种事经常发生，现在又有一件天价物品即将上拍。不，不是Air Jordan球鞋，也不是NFL球星的超级碗戒指。这件体育史上的遗物更加重量级。

本月拍卖的是一件属于绝对传奇人物的球衣——不仅是他的项目传奇，更是所有体育项目和整个时代的传奇。我小时候，朋友的卧室墙上贴着他的海报。作为一名运动员（虽然缺乏自律，更谈不上天赋，无法进入大联盟），我经常看着那张海报，想知道一个如此年轻的人怎么能成为如此耀眼的明星。

这位神秘人物是谁？他取得了什么成就？为什么他的球衣预计能在拍卖会上拍出天价？继续往下读，了解所有细节，准备好你的支票本——这件拍品将会很快售出。

订阅Reader's Digest的Read Up通讯，获取更多体育新闻、金钱、清洁妙招、幽默、旅行、科技和趣味知识。

这就是贝利在1958年世界杯决赛中穿的球衣，当时这位17岁的巴西足球天才在对阵瑞典的比赛中表现出色。他在比赛中攻入两球，至今仍保持着在世界杯决赛中参赛并进球的最年轻球员纪录。

贝利的本名是埃德松·阿兰特斯·多纳西门托，他保持着足球运动员职业生涯进球最多的纪录。根据吉尼斯世界纪录，1956年至1977年间，贝利在1363场比赛中攻入1279球。而1958年巴西对瑞典的那场比赛堪称经典之战。

决赛结束后，这位深受爱戴的足球明星将球衣送给了队友埃德瓦尔多·阿尔维斯·圣塔·罗萨（被称为迪达），他的家人一直保存到1993年，然后捐赠给了一家博物馆。该博物馆2004年仅以10.5万美元售出，所以现在预计600万美元的拍卖价对于这件著名球衣来说确实是相当大的增值。

但还不是最高纪录。体育纪念品最高价的纪录属于另一件蓝色球衣，这是阿根廷的迭戈·马拉多纳在1986年世界杯四分之一决赛中穿的球衣。由于被称为“上帝之手”球衣——因为马拉多纳被英国队指控（确实如此）在那场比赛中攻入两球的第一球时用手触球，这件传奇球衣在2022年以近900万美元售出。

但贝利的1958年世界杯决赛球衣可能会与马拉多纳的球衣一较高下。世界杯热潮现在正热，其他贝利纪念品经常以数百万美元的价格售出。他的一张1958年球星卡在2022年以133万美元售出，2016年一批2000件贝利物品的拍卖组合以超过400万美元成交。

贝利和马拉多纳都是名副其实的足球传奇，但到目前为止，还没有一件体育纪念品能接近目前的纪录保持者：贝比·鲁斯在1932年世界大赛中穿的著名的“指认击球”球衣。该球衣在2024年以惊人的2400万美元售出。

排行榜下一位是迈克尔·乔丹1998年NBA总决赛第一场的球衣，2022年以超过1000万美元售出。这件标志性的红黑色芝加哥公牛队球衣被称为“最后一舞”球衣，因为它在同名ESPN纪录片中被大量呈现。

拳击传奇穆罕默德·阿里著名的“丛林大战”冠军腰带，他在1974年于现在的刚果民主共和国击败乔治·福尔曼的比赛中获得，也在2022年以620万美元售出。这场比赛对阿里来说是重大复出，他此前因拒绝参加越战而被禁止参加拳击。

当然，你可能正好有600万美元闲钱，我们来帮你花掉它。苏富比将举办这场标志性球衣的拍卖会；竞拍从6月29日开始，7月16日结束，正好是2026年世界杯决赛前三天。如果你正好在寻找一件体育史上的遗物（当然你是的！），可以在苏富比注册竞拍。但请注意：你需要证明自己有财务能力参与竞拍，所以准备好你的银行账户信息。
articles.summary:
Sports memorabilia is big business. Can you think of another market where someone’s sweaty old gym shorts could fetch thousands, even millions, of dollars? It happens all the time, and now there’s news of another big-ticket item that’s hitting the auction block. Nope, it’s not an Air Jordan sneaker or an NFL star’s Super Bowl ring. This piece of sports history is even bigger.

What’s up for sale this month is a jersey that belonged to an absolute legend—not just of his game, but of all sports and all time. When I was a kid, my buddy had a poster of him on his bedroom wall. As an athlete myself (albeit a little lacking in the discipline, let alone talent, to make it to the bigs), I looked at it all the time, wondering how someone so young could be such a huge star.

Who is this mystery person, what did he accomplish and why is his jersey expected to fetch that astronomical price at auction? Read on to find out all the details, and get that checkbook out—this one’s going to sell fast.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more sports news, money, cleaning, humor, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

Which jersey is heading to the auction block?

It’s Pelé’s 1958 World Cup final jersey, which the 17-year-old Brazilian soccer phenom wore during a thrilling match against Sweden. He scored two goals in the match and still holds the record for the youngest player to compete and score in a World Cup final.

Pelé, whose given name is Edson Arantes do Nascimento, also holds the record for the most goals scored by a footballer in his career. Between 1956 and 1977, Pelé scored 1,279 goals in 1,363 games, according to Guinness World Records. And the 1958 Brazil-Sweden match was one for the ages.

After the final, the beloved soccer star gave the jersey to his teammate, Edvaldo Alves Santa Rosa (known as Dida), whose family held on to it until 1993, when they donated it to a museum. The museum sold it in 2004 for just $105,000, so the estimated auction price of $6 million is quite the upgrade for the famous jersey!

Will this be the highest-priced soccer item ever sold?

Not just yet. The record for the highest-priced piece of soccer memorabilia goes to another blue jersey, this one worn by Argentina’s Diego Maradona in a 1986 World Cup quarter-final. Called the “Hand of God” shirt because Maradona was accused (rightly) by England of using his hand to score the first of his two goals during that game, the legendary jersey sold for nearly $9 million in 2022.

But Pelé’s 1958 World Cup final jersey could give Maradona’s a run for its money. World Cup fever is high right now, and other Pelé memorabilia routinely sells in the millions of dollars. One of his 1958 trading cards sold in 2022 for $1.33 million, and an auction lot of 2,000 Pelé items sold in 2016 for more than $4 million.

How does this compare to other sports memorabilia?

Pelé and Maradona are bona fide soccer legends, but so far, no piece of sports memorabilia has come close to the current record holder: Babe Ruth’s famous “Called Shot” jersey that he wore in the 1932 World Series. That sold in 2024 for an unbelievable $24 million.

Next on the most-expensive list is Michael Jordan’s 1998 NBA Finals Game 1 jersey, which sold for more than $10 million in 2022. The iconic red and black Chicago Bulls kit is known as the “Last Dance” jersey because it was featured heavily in an ESPN documentary of the same name.

Boxing legend Muhammad Ali’s famous “Rumble in the Jungle” championship belt, which he won for besting George Foreman in a 1974 fight in what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo, also sold in 2022 for $6.2 million. This bout was a major comeback for Ali, who had previously been barred from boxing for refusing to fight in the Vietnam War.

If you have a cool $6 million, how can you get your hands on Pelé’s jersey?

Sure, it’s possible you have $6 million lying around, and we are here to help you spend it. Sotheby’s will be holding the auction for this iconic jersey; bidding starts on June 29 and ends on July 16, three days before the 2026 World Cup final. If you’re in the market for a piece of sports history (of course you are!), you can register to bid at Sotheby’s. But be aware: You’ll have to prove you have the financial means to bid, so have that bank account information ready.

RELATED:

  • Soccer Superfan Prince William Just Delivered a Pep Talk to England’s World Cup Team—Here’s How He’s Getting Them Ready for a Win
  • The Best Day Trips from Every World Cup City in the U.S.
  • This Historic Wizard of Oz Keepsake Was Just Auctioned Off for $32.5 Million!

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • New York Times: “Pelé’s 1958 World Cup final shirt going to auction with estimated value over $6 million”
  • Sotheby’s: “Pele 1958 FIFA World Cup Final Championship Clinching Match Worn Shirt | Two Goals | Brazil’s First World Cup Championship”
  • Guinness World Records: “Most expensive football (soccer) shirt sold at auction”
  • Guinness World Records: “Most career goals (football)”
  • FIFA: “Maradona, England and The Hand of God”
  • CNN: “Muhammad Ali’s WBC ‘Rumble in the Jungle’ belt bought for $6.18 million by Indianapolis Colts owner”
  • Cllct: “Top 10 most expensive pieces of sports memorabilia ever sold”

Why Americans Say Soccer, Not Football

Sports Terms Now Used Every Day

Why Tennis Balls Are Bright Yellow

The post Would You Pay $6 Million for This Famous Jersey? Here’s What Has Sports Fans Salivating Right Now appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: Here’s Why Buttons and Zippers Are on Different Sides of Clothing for Men and Women
articles.content:
我这一生扣过成千上万颗纽扣。也许有好几万颗。（我是五个孩子的妈妈。）我是一个成年了、有多个学位、大脑功能正常的人，然而第一次抓起丈夫的法兰绒衬衫随手一穿、准备去遛狗时，我站在那里整整30秒，眉头紧皱，双手笨拙得像从未见过纽扣一样。这为什么感觉这么奇怪？我中风了吗？

没有。我只是在经历一个最常见、最习以为常的时尚小怪癖：纽扣和拉链的位置取决于衣服是男款还是女款。我不知道在这些年的穿衣生涯中，我是怎么一直没注意到这一点的。男士的纽扣和拉链在右侧；女士的在左侧。这种做法太标准化了，以至于我们很少去想为什么，甚至都不会注意到，但一旦你开始深挖这个话题，就会发现自己陷入了一段出人意料的有趣历史，涉及阶层政治、军事策略和脆弱皇帝的自尊心。

系好安全带，让我们深入了解细节。（顺便说一句，按扣本身可以在任何一侧，不分性别。）

订阅《读者文摘》的Read Up通讯，获取更多有趣的事实、幽默、清洁、旅行和科技资讯。

事情并非如此——这并非古老的历史。纽扣本身可以追溯到公元前2800年左右，但性别化的侧边位置设置要晚得多。时尚历史学家、作家罗伯特·奥桑特表示：“大多数纽扣服装设计在19世纪末标准化，当时我们从所有服装定制转变为现代生产系统。”在此之前，服装都是为个人量身定制的，任何关于位置的规定都是伦敦和巴黎裁缝店设定的非正式传统。约翰·史密斯是另一位时尚历史学家，也是Poshéle的服装设计副总裁，他将这一惯例追溯到17和18世纪，当时裁缝开始系统性地将其应用于客户。

至于拉链，它们直到20世纪初才被发明。伊莱亚斯·豪在1851年获得了一项早期“自动、连续式服装封闭装置”的专利，但现代拉链直到20世纪初才由吉迪恩·森德巴克完善。然而，拉链直到1930年代才在服装中普及。奥桑特说，到那时，性别化的纽扣惯例已经如此根深蒂固，以至于拉链直接继承了这一惯例：“从右向左盖合的惯例反映了女装的纽扣风格，而男装拉链则相反。”

简短的回答是：阶层、便利和几个世纪以来根深蒂固的习惯。更长的答案涉及大量与纽扣或拉链无关的事情。

对于男性来说，逻辑完全是实用性的。右撇子（大约90%的人口是右撇子）在纽扣或拉链位于右侧时更容易抓握和操作。这对需要快速从外套内侧取武器的士兵和武装人员尤为重要。（据说这种事在过去经常发生？）让惯用手控制纽扣——用拇指和食指捏住纽扣并将其穿过——效率更高。同样的逻辑也适用于裤子的纽扣或拉链位置，但原因嘛，是为了另一种类型的快速解开。

接下来就涉及到阶级歧视和性别歧视了。17和18世纪的富家女性不自己穿衣服；她们有女仆和仆人代劳。面对主人的仆人用自己右手操作纽扣，这意味着纽扣需要缝在衣服的左侧。“这在很大程度上是阶层和实用性的区别，而不是纯粹的美学问题，”史密斯说。换句话说，女装之所以设计成这个样子，完全是为了方便别人帮忙扣纽扣。

奥桑特描述当时的女性服装“更加复杂，有系带的内衣、紧身胸衣、衬裙，一层又一层越来越紧的束缚，所以有人帮忙是必不可少的。”制作这些服装的女裁缝严格遵循指示。“她们永远不会干涉设计、改变位置，”奥桑特说。“这会毁掉整个设计。”

还有一个母乳喂养理论在流传：左侧的纽扣让哺乳母亲在用惯用的右手抱着婴儿时更容易打开上衣。虽然这听起来合理，但作为一个哺乳了近十年的妈妈，我对这一说法持保留态度。你需要两侧都哺乳，否则会痛苦地涨奶（而且会变得不对称），而且大多数母亲会交替从哪一侧开始哺乳，所以纽扣位置其实是无关紧要的。另外，我更喜欢用左手抱着婴儿，这样右手（惯用手）就能空出来。

“我最喜欢的替代理论之一是，纽扣放在左侧是因为侧骑时风如何吹过女性身体，”奥桑特解释道，女性通常将双腿放在马的左侧，所以当她们向前骑时，风会把从右侧开口的衬衫吹开。“所以一些人认为门襟（带纽扣孔的布料）和纽扣被调换了，以确保侧骑的女性不用担心风吹开衣服，”他说。我觉得言情小说作家可以用这个创意大做文章。

如果你喜欢有戏剧性的历史，奥桑特提供了他最喜欢的另一个（虽然不得不承认是相当牵强的）替代理论：“据称拿破仑被女性嘲笑他'右手塞进衬衫'的造型激怒了，于是下令将女装翻转，这样她们就无法再嘲笑他了。”

这是真的吗？可能不是。但它很有趣吗？绝对是的。

无论起源如何，工业革命将这些惯例永久地固定下来了。当服装在19世纪末从定制转向大规模生产时，工厂需要明确、可重复的规则和图案。“这是在欧洲范围内制服标准化以提高军队效率的背景下出现的，”奥桑特解释道。“时尚行业只是照搬了。”

一旦这些规则被编入机器，命运就注定了——字面意义上的。

诚实的答案是习惯……以及人类非凡的能力——在有人指出之前什么都注意不到。（我有罪！）奥桑特说：“我们保留这种性别化的闭合系统主要是因为人们习惯了。”“这是大多数人在翻转之前不会注意到的事情，一旦翻转就会困惑。”

史密斯还补充了一个实际因素。“重新调整生产线需要花钱，”他解释道，“只要大多数消费者没有主动抱怨，品牌就几乎没有财务动机去改变。”

还有一个品牌识别的因素，特别是在外套方面。“拉链或一组纽扣的位置是服装的签名特征，”史密斯解释道。“想想标志性的摩托车夹克：不对称的中置拉链和任何logo一样具有辨识度。改变它就像是改变了产品的DNA。”

设计学院进一步强化了这一传统。两位专家都证实，纽扣和拉链位置在服装结构课程中作为技术标准教授。“设计学院教授已建立的性别化方法来插入拉链闭合或纽扣前襟，因为这是一个既定的规范，是人们所期望的，”奥桑特说。他补充了一个个人趣事。他有一件男式衬衫，不小心被做成了“错误”的纽扣方向。“每次穿上它都会觉得有点困难，因为我们太习惯于分配给我们性别的标准方式了。”所以如果连一位完全了解这一惯例的时尚专业人士都会出错，我们普通人就更没机会了。

是的，这就是女性发现自己一辈子都在吃亏的部分。右撇子会发现男装的纽扣位置更符合直觉：惯用的右手握住并引导纽扣，左手握住门襟。对于女装来说，这种关系是反过来的，这意味着右撇子女性每次穿衣时都在与自己的自然灵巧性稍微作对。

“历史系统的优势是人体工程学，”史密斯说。“每件服装都为穿着者和穿着方式进行了优化。”本质上，女装是为右撇子仆人优化的，而不是为穿着它的右撇子女性优化的。

左撇子的人也有自己版本的这种挫折感，他们按同样的逻辑会觉得女装稍微更符合直觉。史密斯指出，对于拉链来说，拉链轨道的方向对舒适度和便利性至关重要。“在结构化皮革服装上，一个僵硬或位置别扭的拉链用起来真的很烦人，尤其是当皮革还是新的、还没有磨合好的时候。”

与此同时，右撇子女性只是适应了。“这只是女性在日常生活中面临的另一个恼人的不便，”奥桑特说，这可能是迄今为止写下的关于时尚历史最轻描淡写的句子。这个特别的怪癖可能还没有达到女装口袋差距的著名程度，但它们确实是同一家族的。

缓慢地，是的。奥桑特预测“更多服装将被设计成中性款式，为占人口90%的右撇子服务。”史密斯同样乐观：“我们已经在市场上看到了这一点。高端设计师和外衣品牌越来越多地提供带有居中拉链、可翻转闭合或明确性别中立硬件位置的款式。”

包括古驰和斯特拉·麦卡特尼在内的大型时装公司都推出了性别中性或无性别系列，该品类在整个行业持续增长。“随着消费者越来越大声地表达他们想要适合自己实际生活方式的服装，而不是传统规定的服装，行业将会跟进，”史密斯说。

也就是说，不要指望这个周末去购物时发现一切都神奇地翻转了。制作服装的机器已经建好了。图案已经标准化了。复古服装已经在强化这一标准。而且很大一部分人口已经花了几十年时间将伸手方向内化了。正如奥桑特坦率地所说：“男性从来没有、也永远不会感到不便。而右撇子女性却不得不忍受一辈子稍微不便，然后又要习惯翻转。”

所以当我抓起丈夫的衬衫时的困惑并不是什么成年失败。这是几个世纪以来仆人、士兵、工业机械，也许还有一个非常小气的拿破仑共同作用的结果。但进步正在到来。与此同时，慰藉一下自己：你现在有了一个在派对上真正有趣的话题可聊。

关于专家

在《读者文摘》，我们致力于为在各自领域具有专业知识和经验的作家生产高质量内容，并咨询相关合格专家。我们依赖可靠的原始来源，包括政府和专业组织、学术机构，以及作家在适当情况下的个人经历。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可信的来源支持，并随着时间推移进行复查，以确保它们保持准确和最新。阅读更多关于我们团队、贡献者和编辑政策的信息。

这篇文章《这就是为什么男女服装上的纽扣和拉链在不同侧》首次发表于《读者文摘》。
articles.summary:
I have buttoned thousands of shirts in my life. Maybe tens of thousands. (I am a mom of five.) I am a grown adult with multiple degrees and a functioning brain, and yet the first time I grabbed my husband’s flannel to throw on before walking the dog, I stood there for a full 30 seconds, brow furrowed, hands fumbling like I’d never encountered a button before. Why did it feel so strange? Was I having a stroke?

No. I was just experiencing one of the most common and most casually accepted little fashion quirks: the fact that buttons and zippers are on opposite sides depending on whether the clothing is designed for men or women. I don’t know how, in all my years of dressing myself, I’d never noticed it before. Men’s buttons and zippers go on the right side; women’s go on the left. It’s so standard that we rarely think to ask why or even notice it, and yet once you start pulling on that thread, you end up in a surprisingly interesting tangle of history, class politics, military strategy and fragile emperor egos.

Buckle up, and let’s dive into the details. (The buckle, for what it’s worth, can go on either side, regardless of your gender.)

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more fun facts, humor, cleaning, travel and tech all week long.

Have buttons and zippers always been like this?

Not exactly—this isn’t ancient history. Buttons themselves date back to around 2800 BCE, but the gendered side placement is much more recent. According to fashion historian and author Robert Ossant, “most button clothing design became standardized in the late 19th century, when we shifted from all clothing being bespoke to the modern system of production.” Before that, clothing was made to measure for individuals, and any rules about placement were more informal traditions set by the tailoring houses of London and Paris. John Smith, a fellow fashion historian and the vice president of fashion design at Poshéle, traces the convention to the 17th and 18th centuries, when tailors began systematically applying it to their clientele.

As for zippers, they weren’t invented until the beginning of the 20th century. Elias Howe patented an early “Automatic, Continuous Clothing Closure” in 1851, but the modern zipper wasn’t perfected until Gideon Sundback did it in the early 1900s. They weren’t common in clothing, however, until the 1930s. By then, Ossant says, the gendered button convention was already so established that zippers simply inherited it: “The convention of right over left for the concealing flap reflected the button style for womenswear, and the opposite for zips used in menswear.”

Why are buttons and zippers on different sides for men and women?

The short answer: class, convenience and a few centuries of deeply entrenched habit. The longer answer involves a remarkable number of things that have nothing to do with buttons or zippers.

Men needed quick access—to weapons

For men, the logic was entirely practical. Right-handed men (and about 90% of the population is right-handed) could more easily grip and manipulate a button or zipper when it was positioned on the right. This was especially important for soldiers and armed men who needed to quickly reach inside their coats for weapons. (Which apparently happened a lot back in the day?) Having the dominant hand control the buttons—holding the button between the thumb and forefinger and pushing it through—was simply more efficient. And the same logic applied to the button or zipper placement on pants, but for, ahem, a different type of quick release.

Wealthy women had someone to dress them

Here’s where it gets classist and sexist. Wealthy women of the 17th and 18th centuries didn’t dress themselves; they had ladies’ maids and servants to do it for them. A servant facing her mistress would be using her own right hand to manipulate the buttons, which meant the buttons needed to be on the left side of the garment. “It was largely a class and practicality distinction rather than a purely aesthetic one,” says Smith. In other words, the entire reason women’s clothing is designed the way it is was to make it easier for someone else to do the buttoning.

Ossant describes women’s clothing of the time as “more complicated, with lace-up undergarments, corsets, petticoats, added in ever more restrictive layers, so having someone help was essential.” The seamstresses who made these garments followed instructions to the letter. “[They] would never interfere with a design and change the placement,” Ossant says. “This would ruin the entire design.”

There’s also a breastfeeding theory floating around: that left-side buttons allowed nursing mothers to more easily open their tops while holding an infant in their dominant right arm. While it’s plausible, as a mom who nursed babies for nearly a decade, I’m side-eyeing this one. You need to breastfeed on both sides or you will become painfully engorged (and lopsided), and most women alternate which side they start with, so button placement is a moot point. Also, I preferred to hold my baby in my left hand so my right (dominant) hand was free.

Horseback riding may have played a role

“One of my favorite alternative theories is that the buttons were placed on the left due to how the wind passed over a woman’s body while riding sidesaddle,” Ossant says, explaining that women typically placed both their legs over the left side of the horse, so as they rode forward, the wind would pull open a blouse that opened to the right. “So some believe the placket (the fabric with the buttonholes) and buttons were swapped to ensure a woman riding sidesaddle on a horse didn’t have to worry about the wind blowing open her clothing,” he says. I feel like romance writers could really do a lot with this idea.

And then there’s Napoleon

If you prefer your history with a side of drama, Ossant offers another one of his favorite (if admittedly far-fetched) alternative theories: “Napoleon was alleged to have been enraged by women mocking his ‘right arm tucked in shirt’ look and ordered that women’s clothing was reversed so they could no longer mock him.”

Is this true? Probably not. Is it delightful? Absolutely.

Mass manufacturing locked it all in

Whatever the origins, the Industrial Revolution cemented these conventions permanently. When clothing shifted from bespoke to mass-produced in the late 19th century, factories needed clear, repeatable rules and patterns. “This came on the back of European-wide uniform standardization to make armies more efficient,” Ossant explains. “The fashion industry simply copied.”

Once those rules were built into the machines, the die was cast—literally.

OK, fine, but why is clothing still like that?

The honest answer is habit … and also the remarkable human capacity to not notice something until it’s pointed out. (Guilty!) “We have kept the gendered closure system mostly because people are used to it,” Ossant says. “It’s something most people don’t notice until it’s flipped, and then they’re confused.”

Smith adds a practical component as well. “Retooling production lines costs money,” he explains, “and as long as the majority of consumers aren’t actively complaining, brands have little financial incentive to change.”

There’s also a brand-identity element, particularly in outerwear. “The placement of a zipper or a set of buttons is part of the garment’s signature,” Smith explains. “Think of iconic motorcycle jackets: The asymmetric off-center zip is as recognizable as any logo. Changing it would feel like altering the product’s DNA.”

Design schools further reinforce the tradition. Both experts confirm that button and zipper placement is taught as a technical standard in garment construction curricula. “Design schools teach the established gendered method for inserting zip closures or button-up fronts because this is now an established norm and what people expect,” says Ossant, who adds a personal anecdote. He owns a man’s shirt that accidentally had its button closure made the “wrong” way. “Every time I put it on, it proves a little difficult because we are so used to the standard way assigned to our genders,” he says. So if even a fashion professional with full knowledge of the convention trips up, the rest of us never had a chance.

Is one side easier than the other to button and zip?

Yes, and this is the part where women discover they’ve been getting a raw deal their entire lives. Right-handed people will find men’s button placement more intuitive: The dominant right hand holds and guides the button, while the left hand holds the placket. For women’s clothing, that dynamic is reversed, meaning right-handed women are working slightly against their natural dexterity every single time they get dressed.

“The advantage of the historical system was ergonomics,” Smith says. “Each garment was optimized for who was fastening it and how.” And, essentially, women’s clothing was optimized for a right-handed servant, not the right-handed woman wearing it.

Left-handed people get their own version of this frustration, and they tend to find women’s garments slightly more intuitive by the same logic. And with zippers, Smith notes, the direction of the zip track matters enormously for comfort and ease. “A stiff or awkwardly placed zip on a structured leather garment is genuinely frustrating to use, especially when the leather is new and hasn’t yet broken in.”

Meanwhile, right-handed women have simply adapted. “It’s just another annoying inconvenience women face in everyday life,” Ossant says, in what may be the most understated sentence ever written about fashion history. This particular quirk may not rise to the level of the famous pocket disparity in women’s clothing, but it’s very much in the same family.

Will this fashion quirk ever change?

Slowly, yes. Ossant predicts that “more clothing [will be] designed as unisex and serve the 90% dominant right-handed population.” Smith is similarly optimistic: “We’re already seeing it in the market. High-end designers and outerwear brands are increasingly offering styles with centered zips, reversible closures or explicitly gender-neutral hardware placement.”

Major fashion houses, including Gucci and Stella McCartney, have launched gender-neutral or unisex lines, and the category has been growing steadily across the industry. “As consumers become more vocal about wanting clothing that fits how they actually live rather than how tradition dictates, the industry will follow,” Smith says.

That said, don’t expect to go shopping this weekend and find everything magically flipped. The machines that make the garments are already built. The patterns are already standardized. Vintage clothing already enforces this standard. And a significant portion of the population has spent decades internalizing which way to reach. As Ossant candidly says: “Men have never and would never have been inconvenienced. Whereas right-handed women have had to put up with a lifetime of it being slightly inconvenient, followed by an era of having to get used to flipping.”

So my confusion when I grabbed my husband’s shirt is not some kind of adulting failure. It is the result of several centuries of servants, soldiers, industrial machinery and possibly one very petty Napoleon. But progress is coming. In the meantime, take comfort in the fact that you now have a genuinely interesting thing to say at parties.

About the experts

  • John Smith is a fashion historian and the vice president of fashion design at Poshéle, where he specializes in garment construction, outerwear and the cultural history of fashion.
  • Robert Ossant is a fashion historian and the author of The Art of Couture Embroidery. He’s also a luxury fashion specialist who has been featured in Vogue, Marie Claire and other fashion magazines.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date.
